sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state
ascii_grid = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')

print("=== Key colored centers ===")
for r in range(len(ascii_grid)):
    for c in range(len(ascii_grid[r])):
        ch = ascii_grid[r][c]
        if ch in 'MO':
            print(f"  ({r},{c}): {ch}")

print("\n=== White pixels ===")
for r in range(len(ascii_grid)):
    for c in range(len(ascii_grid[r])):
        if ascii_grid[r][c] == 'w':
            print(f"  ({r},{c}): w")

# Check what changed
before = last_transition.before_frame.ascii.split('\n')
after = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\n=== Changed cells ===")
for r in range(len(after)):
    for c in range(len(after[r])):
        if r < len(before) and c < len(before[r]) and after[r][c] != before[r][c]:
            print(f"  ({r},{c}): {before[r][c]} -> {after[r][c]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

The gray diamond at (16-20, 55-59) turned white (G→W). This is the same diamond that was completed earlier. So clicking (17, 55) reversed the completion of the diamond. This doesn't seem right.
Wait, let me reconsider. The orange object is still at (14-18, 49-53) with M at (16, 51). The gray diamond at (16-20, 55-59) turned white again.

This seems like the white path that was previously there is now turning gray, and the gray diamond is turning white. This is confusing.
Let me reconsider the mechanics. Maybe the orange object needs to reach a specific position, and clicking white pixels moves it. The white pixels are the path the orange object can take.
Looking at the current state, the orange object is at (14-18, 49-53) with M at (16, 51). The white pixels include (17, 55) which is to the right.
But clicking (17, 55) caused the gray diamond to turn white and some white pixels to turn gray. This seems like a reversal.
